name: Johnny Coles
abstract: John Coles (July 3, 1926 – December 21, 1997) was an American jazz trumpeter.

concerts.label: Miles Davis with Gil Evans and Orchestra
Jazz: The Personal Dimension: Herbie Hancock Sextet
Kool Jazz Festival: A Night of Genius-Artists Celebrate Mingus

about_quotes.speaker: Gary Smulyan
about_quotes.evidence: And there was a band called the Philip Morris Superband... and it was Ray Brown and Jeff Hamilton played the first year on drums, and James Moody and Herb Ellis and Urbie Green and Johnny Coles and Sweets Edison and Jerry Dodgion. I mean it was an amazing band. And we went on the road for three mont

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: I recommend Johnny Coles... We’ve got this rhythm-and-blues band... Tadd hired me, Clifford Brown, Johnny Coles, Cecil Payne, Jymie Merritt, Philly Joe Jones, and a trombone player. We were playing music for the show.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: I had Martin Banks, Virgil Jones, and Johnny Coles on trumpets.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Segal
about_quotes.evidence: That’s where I met Johnny Coles... They were all in that band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Segal — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee initially mentions 'Johnny' in a list of trumpet players but immediately corrects himself to 'Jon [?Faddis]' when the interviewer suggests Johnny Coles. There is no evidence of a direct professional or personal relationship with Johnny Coles in the text.
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Pete Levin
about_quotes.evidence: a lot of great soloists were in that band... Johnny Coles... I think was in the band at that point.
about_quotes.doc_title: Pete Levin interviewed by Monk Rowe, Danbury, Connecticut, November 20, 2001
